name: Next.js
label: Next.js
concurrency: Concurrent rendering via React Suspense + parallel RSC data fetch
memory: GC — V8. RSC payload + per-request state in server heap
securityPosture: Server exposed, no built-in auth, CSP manual
whenToUse: React team, SEO, mixed static + dynamic
whenNot: Pure static → Astro. Mobile → Expo
tradeoff: vs Remix: bigger ecosystem, weaker form handling

name: Remix
label: Remix
concurrency: Sequential loader per route — defer() for non-blocking secondary data
memory: GC — V8. Loader state per request — released after response
securityPosture: Server exposed, web-standard fetch, no built-in auth
whenToUse: Form-heavy apps, progressive enhancement
whenNot: Complex data-fetching graphs → Next.js
tradeoff: vs Next.js: simpler data model, smaller ecosystem

name: Nuxt
label: Nuxt
concurrency: Sequential load() per route — ReadableStream for streaming
memory: GC — V8. Per-request server state in Nitro server heap
securityPosture: Server exposed, Nitro server, no built-in auth
whenToUse: Vue team, full-stack Vue apps
whenNot: React team → Next.js
tradeoff: vs Next.js: Vue ecosystem, smaller community
hydration: Full — partial hydration via islands plugin

name: SvelteKit
label: SvelteKit
concurrency: Sequential load() per route — ReadableStream for streaming
memory: GC — V8. Per-request state — no virtual DOM overhead
securityPosture: Server exposed, adapter-based, no built-in auth
whenToUse: Smallest bundle, compile-time reactivity
whenNot: Large React team → Next.js
tradeoff: vs Next.js: smaller bundle, smaller community
hydration: Full — minimal overhead, no virtual DOM

name: Angular SSR
label: Angular SSR
concurrency: Zone.js change detection — Signals (v17+) for fine-grained updates
memory: GC — V8. Zone.js patch tree + component tree in heap
securityPosture: Strong DI system, CSP requires config, no built-in auth
whenToUse: Enterprise Java-background teams, strict structure
whenNot: Small team, fast iteration → Next.js
tradeoff: vs Next.js: more opinionated, heavier DX overhead

name: Solid Start
label: Solid Start
concurrency: Single-thread async (V8) — fine-grained reactive graph, no virtual DOM diffing
memory: GC — V8. Per-request server state — fine-grained signals, lower heap than React hydration
securityPosture: Server exposed, no built-in auth — fine-grained reactivity unusual attack model
whenToUse: SolidJS team wanting SSR — max performance + React-like syntax
whenNot: Large team expecting React ecosystem → Next.js
tradeoff: vs Next.js: faster rendering via fine-grained signals, smaller ecosystem

name: React
label: React
concurrency: Single render cycle — Concurrent Mode via useTransition / Suspense
memory: GC — V8. Virtual DOM fiber tree in browser heap
securityPosture: XSS via dangerouslySetInnerHTML, no built-in auth
whenToUse: Rich UI, large team, React ecosystem
whenNot: SEO-critical public site → SSR
tradeoff: vs Vue: larger community, higher learning curve

name: Vue
label: Vue
concurrency: Single-thread async (V8 event loop) — reactive proxy system
memory: GC — V8. Reactive proxy tree in browser heap
securityPosture: XSS if v-html misused, no built-in auth
whenToUse: Gentler learning curve, progressive adoption
whenNot: Large enterprise React team → React
tradeoff: vs React: gentler DX, smaller job market

name: Angular
label: Angular
concurrency: Zone.js change detection — Signals (v17+) for fine-grained updates
memory: GC — V8. Zone.js patch tree + component tree in heap
securityPosture: Strong DI, built-in sanitization, no built-in auth
whenToUse: Enterprise, Java-background teams, opinionated structure
whenNot: Small team, fast prototyping → Vue / React
tradeoff: vs React: more structured, heavier toolchain

name: Svelte
label: Svelte
concurrency: Compiler-based reactivity — no virtual DOM, no diffing
memory: GC — V8. No virtual DOM — compiled signals, lower heap use
securityPosture: XSS if {@html} misused, no virtual DOM attack surface
whenToUse: Smallest bundle, compile-time reactivity, no runtime overhead
whenNot: Large team expecting React patterns → React
tradeoff: vs React: near-zero runtime, much smaller community

name: Solid.js
label: Solid.js
concurrency: Fine-grained reactive graph — no virtual DOM, no diffing
memory: GC — V8. Fine-grained reactive graph — no virtual DOM
securityPosture: XSS if innerHTML misused, fine-grained reactivity model
whenToUse: Maximum runtime performance, React-like syntax
whenNot: Large team, ecosystem maturity needed → React
tradeoff: vs React: faster runtime, tiny ecosystem

name: Preact
label: Preact
concurrency: Single render cycle — preact/compat enables React Concurrent features via compatibility layer
memory: GC — V8. 3 kB virtual DOM — 20× smaller heap footprint than React's 45 kB runtime
perf: TTI p99: ~0.8s on 4G (3 kB runtime vs React's 45 kB)
securityPosture: XSS via dangerouslySetInnerHTML (React-compatible API) — same risks as React
whenToUse: Performance-critical apps, embedded widgets, mobile web, React migration path
whenNot: Full React ecosystem (hooks libraries, tooling) needed → React
tradeoff: vs React: 20× smaller runtime, React-compatible via compat layer
renderingModes: CSR only (add preact-iso or Fresh for SSR)

name: Lit
label: Lit
concurrency: Single-thread async (V8) — native browser custom element lifecycle, no virtual DOM
memory: GC — V8. Native Web Component lifecycle — shadow DOM per component, minimal framework heap
perf: TTI p99: ~0.5s on 4G (6 kB runtime, native browser APIs)
securityPosture: Shadow DOM isolation per component — XSS contained within shadow root
whenToUse: Shareable UI components across frameworks, design systems, micro-frontends
whenNot: Single-framework team with React/Vue chosen → use that framework's component model
tradeoff: vs React: native browser standard — components work in any framework or vanilla HTML
hydration: N/A — native browser custom elements, no hydration step
runtimeTarget: Browser (framework-agnostic — works in React, Vue, Angular, vanilla)

name: Astro
label: Astro
concurrency: Build-time only (Vite) — zero runtime concurrency model
memory: GC — V8 (build only). Zero runtime heap — static files served
securityPosture: Zero JS by default, no attack surface at runtime
whenToUse: Content sites, docs, blogs — max performance
whenNot: Highly dynamic data per user → SSR
tradeoff: vs Next.js SSG: zero JS default, fewer integrations
renderingModes: SSG primary — SSR available via adapter

name: Eleventy
label: Eleventy
concurrency: Build-time only (Node.js) — zero runtime concurrency model
memory: GC — V8 (build only). Zero runtime heap
securityPosture: Zero JS by default
whenToUse: Maximum control, any template language
whenNot: React/Vue team → Astro
tradeoff: vs Astro: simpler, no components model

name: Hugo
label: Hugo
concurrency: Build-time only (Go binary) — zero runtime concurrency model
memory: Manual (Go — no GC at build). Go binary compiled, zero runtime heap
securityPosture: Zero JS by default, Go binary — no npm supply chain
whenToUse: Largest site (10k+ pages), fastest build time
whenNot: JS team, component model needed → Astro
tradeoff: vs Astro: 100× faster builds, no JS component ecosystem

name: Gatsby
label: Gatsby
concurrency: Build-time only (V8 + GraphQL layer) — React hydration in browser
memory: GC — V8 (build only). React hydration in browser heap at runtime
securityPosture: GraphQL layer, plugin ecosystem risks
whenToUse: React + GraphQL data layer, image optimization
whenNot: No GraphQL need → Astro or Next.js SSG
tradeoff: vs Astro: GraphQL data layer, slower builds

name: Astro
label: Astro
concurrency: Build-time (Vite) + island-per-component lazy hydration — no shared runtime state
memory: GC — V8. Per-island heap only — static HTML areas have zero runtime overhead
bundleSize: ~0 kb baseline + island size per island
securityPosture: Per-island isolation, zero JS surfaces by default
whenToUse: Content-heavy sites needing selective interactivity
whenNot: Fully dynamic app → SvelteKit or Next.js
tradeoff: vs Next.js: zero-JS baseline, islands only hydrate on demand

name: Fresh
label: Fresh
concurrency: Single-thread async (Deno / V8) — island components hydrate independently
memory: GC — V8 (Deno). Preact component tree per island
securityPosture: No npm by default — smaller supply chain surface
whenToUse: Deno-native stack, edge-first, islands model
whenNot: npm ecosystem needed → Astro
tradeoff: vs Astro: Deno-native, smaller ecosystem

name: Qwik
label: Qwik
concurrency: Single-thread async (V8) + lazy-loaded event handlers per component
memory: GC — V8. Serialized state in HTML — minimal JS heap at load, lazy loaded
perf: TTI p99: <100ms regardless of app size
bundleSize: <1 kb initial (lazy loads on first interaction)
securityPosture: Serialized closures — unusual attack model, no built-in auth
whenToUse: Large apps where hydration cost is a performance bottleneck
whenNot: Small app where hydration cost is negligible → React / Svelte
tradeoff: vs Next.js: near-zero TTI, steep mental model shift
hydration: Resumable — not hydration. <1 kb JS on load regardless of app size

name: Next.js Edge Runtime
label: Next.js Edge Runtime
concurrency: V8 Isolate — no shared memory between requests, event loop per isolate
memory: GC — V8 (128 MB limit). Per-isolate heap — no shared state
securityPosture: Reduced attack surface (no Node.js), no fs/crypto
whenToUse: Personalized pages needing global low latency
whenNot: Node.js APIs needed → Node.js runtime
tradeoff: vs Node.js runtime: 10× faster TTFB, 128 MB limit
renderingModes: SSR (edge) — pages opt-in via export runtime='edge'

name: Hono
label: Hono
concurrency: V8 Isolate (Cloudflare) or Single-thread async (Deno / Bun / Node)
memory: GC — V8. Near-zero framework heap — no class instantiation
securityPosture: Minimal surface — no framework magic, web-standard APIs only
whenToUse: Ultra-light API or SSR at the edge
whenNot: Full-stack app with complex routing → Next.js
tradeoff: vs Next.js: lighter, multi-runtime, no React
hydration: None (HTML only) or full if JSX used
runtimeTarget: Cloudflare Workers, Deno Deploy, Bun, Node.js

name: Remix (Cloudflare)
label: Remix (Cloudflare)
concurrency: V8 Isolate — web-standard fetch, no Node.js event loop
memory: GC — V8 (128 MB limit). Loader state per request
securityPosture: Web-standard fetch, no Node.js leakage at edge
whenToUse: Form-heavy apps needing global low latency
whenNot: Node.js-specific packages needed → Node.js adapter
tradeoff: vs Next.js edge: web-standard model, Cloudflare-native

name: Next.js App Router
label: Next.js App Router
concurrency: Concurrent rendering via React Suspense + parallel RSC data fetch — streaming via ReadableStream
memory: GC — V8. React Suspense boundary state per request
securityPosture: Server components isolated, no client exposure of server code
whenToUse: Large pages where some sections load slower than others
whenNot: Simple page → SSG
tradeoff: vs Pages Router: streaming + RSC, steeper migration
renderingModes: SSR streaming — Suspense boundaries define chunks

workflows.name: 03 PR Build + Scan — Next.js App Router 16
04 Build Push Sign — Next.js App Router 16

name: Remix
label: Remix
concurrency: Sequential loader per route — defer() wraps slow loaders for streaming
memory: GC — V8. Loader state — deferred promises released after response
securityPosture: Web-standard response streaming
whenToUse: Deferred non-critical data, fast initial render
whenNot: Simple page → SSG
tradeoff: vs Next.js: simpler defer model, smaller ecosystem
renderingModes: SSR streaming — defer() wraps slow loaders

name: SvelteKit
label: SvelteKit
concurrency: Single-thread async + ReadableStream — streaming via native Node.js stream
memory: GC — V8. No virtual DOM — streaming state released per chunk
securityPosture: No virtual DOM — smaller runtime attack surface
whenToUse: Smallest streamed bundle, Svelte ecosystem
whenNot: React team → Next.js
tradeoff: vs Next.js: smaller bundle, React incompatible

name: Module Federation
label: Module Federation
concurrency: Browser event loop per remote — each remote loads independently
memory: GC — V8. Each remote has own module heap — shared dependencies deduped
securityPosture: Remote code injection risk — must validate remote origins
whenToUse: Multiple teams, independent deployments, shared dependencies
whenNot: Single team, small app → monorepo SPA
tradeoff: vs iframes: shared state possible, harder to isolate

workflows.name: 03 PR Build + Scan — Module Federation Webpack 5
04 Build Push Sign — Module Federation Webpack 5

name: Single-spa
label: Single-spa
concurrency: Browser event loop — orchestrator pattern, single active app at a time
memory: GC — V8. Shared global scope — all apps share one browser heap
securityPosture: Shared global scope — XSS in one app affects all
whenToUse: Framework-agnostic composition (React + Vue + Angular together)
whenNot: All apps same framework → Module Federation
tradeoff: vs Module Federation: framework agnostic, more configuration

name: Rspack (Module Fed. v2)
label: Rspack (Module Fed. v2)
concurrency: Browser event loop per remote — same composition model as Webpack 5 Module Federation
memory: GC — V8. Each remote has own module heap — shared dependencies deduped via Module Federation v2
perf: 2–10× faster builds than Webpack 5 (Rust-based compiler — same runtime overhead as Webpack)
bundleSize: Shell ~10 kb + each remote independently (identical to Webpack 5 Module Federation)
securityPosture: Remote code injection risk — same as Webpack Module Federation; must validate remote origins
scaling: Horizontal (each remote deployed and scaled independently)
whenToUse: Module Federation teams hitting slow Webpack 5 build times — Rspack is a drop-in replacement with 2–10× faster builds
whenNot: Build speed not a bottleneck → stay on Webpack 5 Module Federation
tradeoff: vs Webpack 5 Module Federation: identical runtime behavior and config API, 2–10× faster builds due to Rust implementation
renderingModes: CSR (primary) — SSR support via Module Federation v2 SSR mode

workflows.name: 03 PR Build + Scan — Module Federation Rspack 1
04 Build Push Sign — Module Federation Rspack 1

name: React Native
label: React Native
concurrency: JS thread + native UI thread — new arch: JSI near-synchronous bridge
memory: GC — Hermes (Meta's JS engine). Pre-compiled bytecode — faster startup than V8
securityPosture: JS bridge — XSS not applicable, native module risks
whenToUse: React team targeting iOS + Android, large native module ecosystem
whenNot: High-performance games or 3D → Flutter / Native
tradeoff: vs Flutter: larger ecosystem, slower UI than Flutter
stateInit: Redux / Zustand / Jotai initialized in App.tsx before first render

name: Expo
label: Expo
concurrency: JS thread + native thread (React Native — same arch) — managed via Expo modules
memory: GC — Hermes. Same as React Native + Expo module heap
securityPosture: Managed workflow limits native attack surface
whenToUse: React Native apps without Xcode/Android Studio setup
whenNot: Deep native customization → bare React Native
tradeoff: vs bare React Native: faster DX, less native control
rendering: Native UI components — React Native under the hood
stateInit: Same as React Native + expo-modules state via Expo managed workflow

name: Ionic
label: Ionic
concurrency: Browser event loop (WebView) — JS-to-native bridge for Capacitor plugins
memory: GC — V8 (WebView). Full web stack heap in native shell
securityPosture: WebView — full web attack surface in a native shell
whenToUse: Web team, web-only UI components, Capacitor plugins
whenNot: Native performance critical → React Native / Flutter
tradeoff: vs React Native: web-native, lower performance ceiling
stateInit: Web framework (React/Vue/Angular) state initialized in WebView DOM context

name: Flutter
label: Flutter
concurrency: Single-thread (Dart isolate) + isolates for background — Impeller GPU render thread
memory: GC — Dart VM. Generational GC per isolate — each isolate has own heap, no global pause
perf: ~120fps (Impeller renderer — GPU direct on supported hardware)
securityPosture: No WebView — smaller attack surface, custom renderer
whenToUse: Pixel-perfect cross-platform UI, animations, games
whenNot: JS team, web output primary → React Native + Expo
tradeoff: vs React Native: faster/smoother UI, Dart is a new language
rendering: Custom GPU renderer (Impeller — draws every pixel directly)

name: .NET MAUI
label: .NET MAUI
concurrency: Thread pool + async/await — native UI thread per platform
memory: GC — CLR (Mobile GC). Xamarin-derived mobile GC. Conservative collection
securityPosture: .NET runtime, no WebView, native controls
whenToUse: .NET / C# team targeting iOS, Android, Windows, macOS
whenNot: No .NET team → Flutter
tradeoff: vs Flutter: C# ecosystem, lower UI flexibility
stateInit: App.xaml.cs OnStart() — dependency injection container initialized before first screen

name: Kotlin Multiplatform
label: Kotlin Multiplatform
concurrency: Coroutines — multiplatform. Native coroutine support on iOS and Android
memory: GC — JVM ART (Android) / experimental K/N GC (iOS). JVM ART: AOT compiled GC
securityPosture: Kotlin Native on iOS — smaller attack surface than JVM
whenToUse: Kotlin Android team adding iOS support, shared business logic
whenNot: UI sharing needed → Flutter
tradeoff: vs Flutter: shares logic not UI, Kotlin native on iOS
rendering: Native UI per platform (Compose Multiplatform for shared UI)
stateInit: ViewModel + StateFlow initialized in Activity.onCreate() (Android) / App entry point (iOS)

name: Swift / SwiftUI
label: Swift / SwiftUI
concurrency: GCD + Swift structured concurrency (async/await) — actors for shared mutable state
memory: ARC (Automatic Reference Counting). No GC pauses. Memory freed when last reference drops
bundleSize: ~5–30 MB IPA (app-dependent, App Store thins per-device)
securityPosture: App Sandbox, keychain, biometrics, no WebView unless needed
whenToUse: iOS-first product, full platform API access, latest iOS features
whenNot: iOS + Android both needed → Flutter or React Native
tradeoff: vs React Native: best iOS perf, iOS-only
stateInit: @StateObject + @AppStorage initialized at App struct launch. @EnvironmentObject injected at root.
runtimeTarget: iOS / iPadOS / macOS / watchOS / tvOS / visionOS

name: Objective-C (UIKit)
label: Objective-C (UIKit)
concurrency: GCD + NSOperationQueue — main thread for all UI, background queues for async work
memory: Manual (MRC) or ARC. ARC is standard since 2011 — same model as Swift
perf: Native — same as Swift (Core Animation / Metal)
securityPosture: Same as Swift sandbox model
whenToUse: Maintaining existing Obj-C codebase
whenNot: New iOS project → Swift
tradeoff: vs Swift: no type safety, verbose syntax, no modern concurrency

name: Kotlin + Jetpack Compose
label: Kotlin + Jetpack Compose
concurrency: Coroutines + Flow — main dispatcher for UI, IO/Default dispatchers for async
memory: GC — JVM ART. Android Runtime with AOT compilation — faster than Dalvik
securityPosture: App Sandbox, Keystore, biometrics
whenToUse: Android-first product, Google ecosystem, modern UI
whenNot: iOS + Android both needed → Flutter or React Native
tradeoff: vs Flutter: native Android perf, Android-only
stateInit: ViewModel + StateFlow initialized in Activity.onCreate() via Hilt or Koin DI

name: Java Android SDK
label: Java Android SDK
concurrency: Thread pool + Java executors — main thread for UI (AsyncTask deprecated)
memory: GC — JVM ART. Same ART runtime as Kotlin
securityPosture: Same Android sandbox model
whenToUse: Maintaining existing Java Android codebase
whenNot: New Android project → Kotlin + Compose
tradeoff: vs Kotlin: no null safety, verbose, no coroutines
stateInit: Variables initialized in Application.onCreate() and Activity.onCreate()

name: Workbox
label: Workbox
concurrency: Service Worker (browser background thread) — intercepts fetch, serves from cache
memory: GC — V8 (Service Worker). Isolated heap — SW failure does not crash the page
perf: Cache hit: <10ms TTFB from SW cache. Cache miss: network-dependent
securityPosture: HTTPS required, Service Worker scope isolation
whenToUse: Adding offline + caching to any existing web app
whenNot: Deep native APIs (Bluetooth, NFC) → Native or Capacitor
tradeoff: vs Capacitor: zero native code, limited native API access
rendering: Any (SSR/SSG/CSR) — Workbox adds Service Worker caching layer on top
runtimeTarget: Browser (installable on iOS Safari / Android Chrome)

name: Vite PWA Plugin
label: Vite PWA Plugin
concurrency: Service Worker (browser background thread) — Workbox underneath
memory: GC — V8. Same as Workbox — isolated SW heap
perf: Same as Workbox — SW cache hit: <10ms, miss: network
securityPosture: HTTPS required
whenToUse: Vite-based app needing PWA without manual Workbox config
whenNot: Non-Vite build system → Workbox directly
tradeoff: vs Workbox: zero-config PWA for Vite, less control
runtimeTarget: Browser (installable on iOS Safari / Android Chrome)

workflows.name: 03 PR Build + Scan — Vite PWA Plugin 0.21
04 Build Push Sign — Vite PWA Plugin 0.21

name: Express
label: Express
concurrency: Single-thread event loop — middleware chain sequential per request
memory: GC — V8. Minimal — middleware chain, no framework heap overhead
securityPosture: No built-in security — needs helmet.js, cors, rate-limit
whenToUse: Largest middleware ecosystem, maximum flexibility
whenNot: High throughput → Fastify
tradeoff: vs Fastify: 2× slower, larger middleware ecosystem

name: Fastify
label: Fastify
concurrency: Single-thread event loop — schema-validated async hook pipeline
memory: GC — V8. Schema cache (~5MB at boot). Request objects pooled
securityPosture: Schema validation built-in (Ajv), helmet recommended
whenToUse: High throughput JSON APIs, schema-first design
whenNot: Non-JSON protocols or Express plugins needed → Express
tradeoff: vs Express: 2× faster, smaller middleware ecosystem

name: NestJS
label: NestJS
concurrency: DI-scoped async handlers — request pipeline via guards + interceptors
memory: GC — V8. DI container + module graph held at boot (~30MB)
securityPosture: Guards, interceptors, pipes — structured auth model
whenToUse: Enterprise, Angular-background team, opinionated structure
whenNot: Simple API → Express or Fastify
tradeoff: vs Express: structured DI + modules, heavier boilerplate

name: Hono
label: Hono
concurrency: Multi-runtime async — same handler runs on Node / Deno / Bun / CF Workers
memory: GC — V8. Near-zero heap — no class instantiation
securityPosture: Minimal surface — web-standard APIs only
whenToUse: Edge + multi-runtime API (Node, Deno, Bun, Cloudflare)
whenNot: Node.js-only middleware ecosystem needed → Fastify
tradeoff: vs Fastify: multi-runtime, no Node.js-specific middleware

name: Deno
label: Deno
concurrency: Single-thread async + Workers API for true parallelism
memory: GC — V8. Permissions state + module cache
securityPosture: Permissions model — deny-by-default filesystem, network, env access
whenToUse: Security-first server, TypeScript without build step
whenNot: npm-heavy codebase → Node.js
tradeoff: vs Node.js: deny-by-default security, smaller npm compat

name: Elysia
label: Elysia
concurrency: Single-thread async — Bun runtime only, JavaScriptCore event loop
memory: GC — JavaScriptCore (Bun). Near-zero heap — value-based routing, no class overhead
perf: p99: ~1ms (Bun runtime, consistently fastest TS framework)
securityPosture: End-to-end type safety via Eden treaty — type errors at compile time
whenToUse: Maximum Bun throughput, end-to-end TypeScript type safety, Eden RPC
whenNot: Non-Bun runtime required → Hono
tradeoff: vs Hono: Bun-only, higher throughput, end-to-end type safety
threadModel: Single-thread async (Bun only — not Node/Deno compatible)

name: Bun (native)
label: Bun (native)
concurrency: Single-thread async — JavaScriptCore event loop (not V8), Bun.serve() built-in HTTP
memory: GC — JavaScriptCore (JSC). Near-zero framework heap — no middleware chain, no DI container
perf: p99: ~1ms (Bun.serve() — consistently fastest JS HTTP baseline)
securityPosture: Minimal surface — no middleware magic, Bun's built-in TLS support
whenToUse: Maximum raw Bun throughput, Bun-first teams, ultra-low latency APIs without framework overhead
whenNot: npm ecosystem compatibility critical or multi-runtime required → Hono or Fastify
tradeoff: vs Elysia: raw Bun.serve() — no framework overhead, no type safety layer — lower DX, higher throughput
threadModel: Single-thread async (Bun / JavaScriptCore — not Node.js or Deno compatible)

name: FastAPI
label: FastAPI
concurrency: Async (asyncio / Starlette) — ASGI interface, supports WebSockets
memory: GC — CPython refcount + cycle collector. Pydantic model cache at boot
securityPosture: Automatic OpenAPI, Pydantic validation, no built-in auth
whenToUse: ML/AI APIs, async Python, auto OpenAPI docs
whenNot: CPU-bound processing → Go or Rust
tradeoff: vs Django: async-first, lighter, no ORM built-in

name: Django
label: Django
concurrency: Sync (WSGI) + async (ASGI) — both supported; ORM is sync-first
memory: GC — CPython. ORM + settings + URL resolver held in heap at boot
securityPosture: CSRF protection, SQL injection via ORM, security middleware built-in
whenToUse: Full-stack web app, admin panel, ORM-heavy projects
whenNot: Lightweight API → FastAPI
tradeoff: vs FastAPI: batteries-included ORM + admin, slower async

name: Flask
label: Flask
concurrency: Sync (WSGI) + async via Quart fork — explicit per-request context
memory: GC — CPython. Near-zero overhead — explicit per-request context
securityPosture: Minimal — no CSRF by default, needs Flask-Login, Flask-WTF
whenToUse: Micro-services, simple APIs, teaching Python web dev
whenNot: Async-first → FastAPI
tradeoff: vs FastAPI: no async, no auto-docs, more control

name: Starlette
label: Starlette
concurrency: Async (asyncio) — ASGI interface. FastAPI is built on top of Starlette
memory: GC — CPython. Minimal — FastAPI is built on top of this
securityPosture: Minimal — FastAPI is built on top of this
whenToUse: Raw ASGI performance, building frameworks on top of
whenNot: Need auto-docs or validation → FastAPI
tradeoff: vs FastAPI: lower-level, faster, no Pydantic

name: Gin
label: Gin
concurrency: Goroutine per request — M:N scheduler across all CPU cores
memory: GC — Go tri-color concurrent. Static binary ~10MB RSS per 1k goroutines
securityPosture: No built-in auth — explicit middleware for CORS, auth
whenToUse: High-throughput REST APIs, Go ecosystem, large community
whenNot: Ultra-low latency → Fiber
tradeoff: vs Echo: larger community, similar performance
threadModel: Goroutines — M:N scheduling (OS threads backing goroutine pool)

name: Echo
label: Echo
concurrency: Goroutine per request — M:N scheduler across all CPU cores
memory: GC — Go tri-color concurrent. Static binary ~10MB RSS per 1k goroutines
securityPosture: Same as Gin — explicit middleware
whenToUse: Cleaner API than Gin, middleware-first design
whenNot: Need Gin's larger plugin ecosystem → Gin
tradeoff: vs Gin: cleaner API, smaller community

name: Fiber
label: Fiber
concurrency: Goroutine per request — fasthttp (bypasses net/http, different pooling)
memory: GC — Go tri-color concurrent. fasthttp pools request objects — lower allocation rate
securityPosture: Deviates from net/http — middleware compatibility issues
whenToUse: Maximum Go HTTP throughput
whenNot: net/http middleware compatibility needed → Gin or Echo
tradeoff: vs Gin: 2× faster, incompatible with net/http middleware

name: Chi
label: Chi
concurrency: Goroutine per request — net/http compatible, M:N scheduler
memory: GC — Go tri-color concurrent. Static binary — minimal overhead
securityPosture: net/http compatible — all standard lib middleware works
whenToUse: Standard library compatibility, minimal abstraction over net/http
whenNot: Need rich middleware ecosystem → Gin
tradeoff: vs Gin: closer to stdlib, less opinionated router

name: Spring Boot
label: Spring Boot
concurrency: Thread pool (MVC, blocking) or Reactor event loop (WebFlux, reactive)
memory: GC — JVM G1GC / ZGC. IoC container ~200MB heap at boot
securityPosture: Spring Security built-in, CSRF, OAuth2, mature ecosystem
whenToUse: Enterprise Java, existing Spring ecosystem, security-critical apps
whenNot: Low startup time → Quarkus native
tradeoff: vs Quarkus: bigger ecosystem, slower startup
threadModel: Thread pool (MVC) or event loop (WebFlux / Netty)

name: Quarkus
label: Quarkus
concurrency: Vert.x reactive event loop + worker thread pool for blocking ops
memory: GC — JVM or GraalVM native (no GC in native mode). Zero heap startup in native
securityPosture: GraalVM native eliminates reflection attack vectors
whenToUse: Kubernetes-native, container-first, fast startup
whenNot: Existing Spring team → Spring Boot
tradeoff: vs Spring Boot: 10× faster startup native, smaller ecosystem

name: Micronaut
label: Micronaut
concurrency: Reactive (Netty) + async — compile-time DI, no reflection at runtime
memory: GC — JVM or GraalVM native. Compile-time DI — no reflection heap
securityPosture: Compile-time DI — no reflection at runtime
whenToUse: Compile-time DI, GraalVM native, low memory
whenNot: Spring familiarity needed → Spring Boot
tradeoff: vs Spring Boot: no reflection, compile-time AOP

name: Ktor
label: Ktor
concurrency: Coroutine per request — Netty event loop underneath
memory: GC — JVM G1GC. Coroutine context objects per request
securityPosture: Explicit plugin model — no magic, auth plugin required
whenToUse: Kotlin-first server, coroutines, JetBrains ecosystem
whenNot: Spring familiarity needed → Spring Boot Kotlin
tradeoff: vs Spring Boot: lightweight, Kotlin-idiomatic, smaller ecosystem

name: Spring Boot (Kotlin)
label: Spring Boot (Kotlin)
concurrency: Coroutines (WebFlux) + thread pool (MVC) — Spring coroutine support on WebFlux
memory: GC — JVM G1GC / ZGC. Spring DI container + coroutine context in heap
securityPosture: Spring Security, full mature security model
whenToUse: Existing Spring Java team migrating to Kotlin
whenNot: New Kotlin project, no Spring baggage → Ktor
tradeoff: vs Ktor: Spring ecosystem, more boilerplate

workflows.name: 03 PR Build + Scan — Spring Boot Kotlin 3.4
04 Build Push Sign — Spring Boot Kotlin 3.4

name: ASP.NET Core
label: ASP.NET Core
concurrency: Async/await on Kestrel thread pool — I/O threads managed by CLR
memory: GC — CLR (Server GC). Server GC tuned for throughput over pause time
securityPosture: Built-in auth, CSRF, HTTPS enforcement, security middleware
whenToUse: .NET team, high-throughput APIs, Windows or Linux
whenNot: No .NET team → Go or Node.js
tradeoff: vs Go: comparable speed, larger enterprise ecosystem
threadModel: Async/await on thread pool (Kestrel I/O threads)

name: Minimal APIs (.NET 9)
label: Minimal APIs (.NET 9)
concurrency: Async/await on Kestrel thread pool — same as ASP.NET Core
memory: GC — CLR. Less startup code = slightly lower initial heap
securityPosture: Same as ASP.NET Core — built on same runtime
whenToUse: Simple APIs without MVC overhead, microservices
whenNot: Complex routing, controllers, auth needed → ASP.NET Core MVC
tradeoff: vs ASP.NET MVC: less boilerplate, less structure

name: Axum
label: Axum
concurrency: Tokio async executor — multi-thread, work-stealing scheduler
memory: Borrow checker — no GC. Zero heap allocation overhead. ~5MB RSS idle
securityPosture: Memory safety at compile time, no null, no buffer overflow
whenToUse: Maximum safety + performance, systems-level services
whenNot: Rapid prototyping, team unfamiliar with Rust → Go
tradeoff: vs Actix-web: Tokio-native, simpler type model

name: Actix-web
label: Actix-web
concurrency: Async (Actix actor system + Tokio) — actor-per-handler concurrency
memory: Borrow checker — no GC. Actix actor heap per actor instance
securityPosture: Same Rust memory safety guarantees
whenToUse: Highest raw HTTP throughput needed
whenNot: Simpler API preferred → Axum
tradeoff: vs Axum: marginally faster, more complex type constraints

name: Phoenix
label: Phoenix
concurrency: Actor model — one BEAM process per connection, millions concurrent
memory: GC — per-process BEAM GC. Each BEAM process has isolated heap — no stop-the-world
securityPosture: CSRF, XSS, SQL injection via Ecto, secure defaults
whenToUse: Real-time apps, WebSockets, chat, IoT — massive concurrency
whenNot: CPU-bound computation → Go or Rust
tradeoff: vs Node.js: 10× more WebSocket connections, fault-tolerant
threadModel: Actor model — millions of isolated BEAM processes, no shared state

name: Rails
label: Rails
concurrency: Thread-per-request (Puma) — GIL limits true CPU parallelism
memory: GC — MRI generational. Per-request object allocation reset via GC
securityPosture: CSRF, XSS, SQL injection via ActiveRecord, strong defaults
whenToUse: Startup speed, convention over config, prototypes, SaaS
whenNot: High throughput → Go or Node.js
tradeoff: vs Sinatra: full-stack batteries, slower throughput

name: Sinatra
label: Sinatra
concurrency: Thread-per-request (Puma) — GIL limits true CPU parallelism
memory: GC — MRI generational. Near-zero framework overhead vs Rails
securityPosture: Minimal — no CSRF by default, explicit config
whenToUse: Simple APIs, microservices, Ruby team wanting minimal framework
whenNot: Full-stack app → Rails
tradeoff: vs Rails: lighter, higher req/s, no conventions

name: Laravel
label: Laravel
concurrency: Process-per-request (PHP-FPM) — no shared memory. Octane (Swoole / RoadRunner) for persistent process
memory: GC — PHP refcount + cycle. Per-request memory fully reset by FPM
securityPosture: CSRF, XSS, SQL injection via Eloquent ORM, strong defaults
whenToUse: Full-stack PHP, large PHP community, rapid prototyping
whenNot: High concurrent connections → Node.js or Go
tradeoff: vs Symfony: faster DX, less flexibility

name: Symfony
label: Symfony
concurrency: Process-per-request (PHP-FPM) — no shared memory between requests
memory: GC — PHP refcount + cycle collector. Per-request memory reset
securityPosture: CSRF, XSS, mature security component
whenToUse: Enterprise PHP, component-based, Drupal/API Platform underneath
whenNot: Speed of delivery primary → Laravel
tradeoff: vs Laravel: more flexible, steeper learning curve

name: Slim
label: Slim
concurrency: Process-per-request (PHP-FPM) — minimal DI overhead
memory: GC — PHP refcount. Near-zero framework overhead — minimal boot
securityPosture: Minimal — PSR-7 standard, explicit middleware only
whenToUse: Micro-APIs, PHP teams wanting minimal abstraction
whenNot: Full-stack needs → Laravel
tradeoff: vs Laravel: 2× faster, no conventions

name: Vapor
label: Vapor
concurrency: Swift structured concurrency (async/await + actors) + SwiftNIO event loop
memory: ARC (Automatic Reference Counting). No GC pauses. ~80MB RSS idle
securityPosture: No built-in auth — Vapor-Auth plugin, Swift type safety
whenToUse: iOS/macOS team extending to server, ARC memory model, no GC pauses
whenNot: Non-Swift team → Node.js or Go
tradeoff: vs Go: ARC vs GC, smaller ecosystem
threadModel: Swift structured concurrency + SwiftNIO event loop

name: Hummingbird
label: Hummingbird
concurrency: Swift structured concurrency + SwiftNIO event loop — lighter than Vapor
memory: ARC. Minimal framework overhead vs Vapor
securityPosture: Minimal surface — explicit middleware only
whenToUse: Higher throughput than Vapor, lighter dependencies
whenNot: Rich plugin ecosystem needed → Vapor
tradeoff: vs Vapor: faster, smaller ecosystem, newer
threadModel: Swift structured concurrency + SwiftNIO event loop

name: Play Framework
label: Play Framework
concurrency: Akka actor system + Netty event loop — reactive by default
memory: GC — JVM G1GC. Akka actor heap — actors hold state across requests
securityPosture: CSRF, XSS protection, mature security helpers
whenToUse: Scala team, reactive web apps, Akka ecosystem
whenNot: No Scala team → Spring Boot
tradeoff: vs Spring Boot: reactive-first, Scala type system

name: http4s
label: http4s
concurrency: Cats Effect fiber scheduler — purely functional green threads on JVM
memory: GC — JVM G1GC. Cats Effect fiber stack — purely functional allocation
securityPosture: Purely functional — no shared mutable state
whenToUse: Pure functional Scala, Cats Effect ecosystem, type-safe HTTP
whenNot: Team unfamiliar with FP → Play
tradeoff: vs Play: pure FP, steeper learning curve

name: Ring
label: Ring
concurrency: Thread pool (Jetty) or async (http-kit) — functional middleware composition
memory: GC — JVM G1GC. Clojure immutable data structures reduce GC pressure
securityPosture: Minimal — explicit middleware model, Clojure immutability reduces state bugs
whenToUse: Clojure team, REPL-driven APIs, functional purity
whenNot: No Clojure team → Go or Python
tradeoff: vs Pedestal: simpler model, less structure

name: Pedestal
label: Pedestal
concurrency: Async interceptor chain — each step explicit, composable, testable
memory: GC — JVM G1GC. Interceptor context map allocated per request
securityPosture: Interceptor chain — each step explicit, no hidden middleware magic
whenToUse: Interceptor-based architecture, async Clojure APIs
whenNot: Simple API → Ring
tradeoff: vs Ring: async interceptors, more structure

name: Drogon
label: Drogon
concurrency: Coroutines + multi-thread event loop (libuv-style)
memory: Manual — smart pointers (shared_ptr / unique_ptr). No GC — deterministic allocation
securityPosture: No memory safety guarantees — manual management, buffer overflow risk
whenToUse: Absolute maximum HTTP throughput, HFT, game server APIs
whenNot: Rapid development team → Go or Rust
tradeoff: vs Rust Axum: faster raw throughput, unsafe memory model

name: Crow
label: Crow
concurrency: Multi-thread async (Boost.Asio thread pool)
memory: Manual — smart pointers. Header-only library — no separate build step
securityPosture: Same C++ manual memory risks
whenToUse: Simpler C++ HTTP API than Drogon, header-only
whenNot: Absolute maximum perf → Drogon
tradeoff: vs Drogon: simpler API, 3× lower throughput

name: grpc-go
label: grpc-go
concurrency: Goroutines — M:N green threads, HTTP/2 multiplexed streams per connection
memory: GC — Go. Per-stream goroutine stack (2 kB initial). Low GC pause — well-suited for long-lived streams
securityPosture: TLS 1.3 built-in, mTLS native, interceptor chain for auth and tracing
buildTool: Go (buf or protoc for proto generation)
whenToUse: Highest-performance Go microservices, service mesh, server + client streaming
whenNot: Browser clients without grpc-web proxy → ConnectRPC or REST
tradeoff: vs REST: 5–10× throughput, binary protocol — not human-readable, requires proto schema
threadModel: Goroutines (M:N green threads — auto-scales to all CPUs)

name: grpc-java
label: grpc-java
concurrency: Async via Netty event loop — non-blocking I/O with Netty NIO transport
memory: JVM heap — Netty ByteBuf pool. JVM base overhead ~100 MB heap
securityPosture: TLS via Netty SSL, mTLS, channel credentials, server interceptors
whenToUse: Java/Spring microservices, existing JVM ecosystem, Android gRPC clients
whenNot: Startup time critical (serverless) → grpc-go or grpc-node
tradeoff: vs grpc-go: comparable warm throughput, higher startup overhead and container size
threadModel: Non-blocking async (Netty NIO — boss/worker thread pool)

name: grpcio
label: grpcio
concurrency: Async (grpc.aio + asyncio) — C-core underlying transport bypasses Python GIL for I/O
memory: Python heap + C-core native memory. GIL still applies for Python handler code
p99Latency: p99: ~1ms unary RPC (Python overhead on C-core)
securityPosture: TLS via C-core (OpenSSL), mTLS, server interceptors
whenToUse: Python ML model serving, data science microservices, consuming gRPC APIs from Python
whenNot: High concurrency required → grpc-go. Performance-critical server → grpc-go
tradeoff: vs grpcio-asyncio: sync grpcio blocks GIL per handler — use grpc.aio for async
threadModel: Async (asyncio + C-core async I/O — GIL limits handler parallelism)

name: @grpc/grpc-js
label: @grpc/grpc-js
concurrency: Single-thread event loop — pure JS transport, no C bindings required
memory: GC — V8. No C-core dependency — pure JavaScript implementation
securityPosture: TLS via Node.js native tls module, mTLS channel credentials, call interceptors
buildTool: Node.js (proto-loader for dynamic proto loading or protoc for static)
whenToUse: Node.js gRPC clients/servers, TypeScript microservices, interop with Go/Java gRPC
whenNot: Native binary performance → grpc-go. Complex bidirectional streaming at scale → grpc-go
tradeoff: vs grpc-go: pure JS — 5× higher latency, same HTTP/2 protocol wire compatibility
threadModel: Single-thread event loop (Node.js — use cluster for multi-core)

name: grpc-dotnet
label: grpc-dotnet
concurrency: Async/await (Task) — Kestrel HTTP/2 server, fully async pipeline
memory: .NET GC — managed heap. Kestrel socket buffers, low framework overhead
securityPosture: TLS via Kestrel, mTLS client certificates, ASP.NET Core auth middleware
whenToUse: .NET microservices, ASP.NET Core APIs replacing REST, Azure workloads
whenNot: Browser clients without grpc-web → REST. Non-.NET clients → standard gRPC + proxy
tradeoff: vs REST (ASP.NET Core): 3× throughput, binary protocol — debugging harder without grpc-ui
threadModel: Async/await (Thread pool — scales to all CPUs)

name: tonic
label: tonic
concurrency: Async (Tokio) — zero-cost abstractions, work-stealing multi-thread runtime
memory: No GC — deterministic allocation. Tokio async runtime near-zero overhead per task
p99Latency: p99: ~0.2ms unary RPC (lowest latency among major gRPC implementations)
securityPosture: TLS via rustls (memory-safe, no OpenSSL CVE risk), mTLS, tower middleware interceptors
buildTool: cargo (tonic-build for proto generation in build.rs)
whenToUse: Lowest latency gRPC, memory safety critical, Rust microservices, embedded service mesh
whenNot: Rapid prototyping → grpc-go. Python/Java team → use that language's gRPC impl
tradeoff: vs grpc-go: lower latency, no GC pauses, higher compile complexity and learning curve
threadModel: Async Tokio (multi-threaded work-stealing — scales to all CPUs)

name: grpc-swift
label: grpc-swift
concurrency: Swift structured concurrency (async/await) — SwiftNIO event loop
memory: ARC — no GC, deterministic memory management, low per-stream overhead
securityPosture: TLS via SwiftNIO SSL (BoringSSL), mTLS, interceptors
whenToUse: iOS/macOS gRPC clients, Vapor server with gRPC, Swift server microservices
whenNot: Non-Apple platform primary → grpc-go. Python/Java team → use their impl
tradeoff: vs grpc-go: native iOS/macOS integration advantage, smaller Linux server ecosystem
threadModel: Swift structured concurrency (SwiftNIO NIO event loop — multi-threaded)

name: grpc-kotlin
label: grpc-kotlin
concurrency: Coroutines — suspending functions, non-blocking on Kotlin coroutine dispatcher
memory: JVM heap — Kotlin coroutines minimal overhead vs Java threads. Netty ByteBuf pool
securityPosture: TLS via Netty SSL, mTLS channel credentials, coroutine-aware interceptors
whenToUse: Kotlin microservices, Android coroutine gRPC clients, Spring Boot Kotlin
whenNot: Pure Java team → grpc-java directly. Performance critical → grpc-go
tradeoff: vs grpc-java: idiomatic Kotlin coroutines, same JVM warm throughput
threadModel: Coroutines (Kotlin coroutine dispatcher — non-blocking on JVM thread pool)

name: connect-go
label: connect-go
concurrency: Goroutines — same as net/http, compatible with gRPC + gRPC-Web + Connect protocols
memory: GC — Go. net/http2 transport, minimal overhead — no separate gRPC runtime
securityPosture: TLS via Go stdlib, mTLS, works with standard HTTP reverse proxies (no envoy required)
scaling: Horizontal (compatible with standard HTTP load balancers)
buildTool: Go (buf generate for proto + connect-go plugin)
whenToUse: gRPC + browser clients without grpc-web proxy, standard HTTP LBs, buf.build ecosystem
whenNot: Existing envoy + raw gRPC only → grpc-go. Non-Go teams
tradeoff: vs grpc-go: HTTP/1.1 + browser compatible without proxy, slightly lower raw throughput
threadModel: Goroutines (M:N green threads — auto-scales to all CPUs)

name: grpc-php
label: grpc-php
concurrency: Synchronous (PHP-FPM) — each request in new FPM worker, no async model
memory: PHP GC — ZendEngine per-request. grpc C extension is C-based native memory
securityPosture: TLS via OpenSSL (grpc C-core), mTLS supported
whenToUse: PHP services consuming gRPC APIs (GCP client libs, Shopify), Laravel gRPC clients
whenNot: PHP as high-traffic gRPC server → use grpc-go as sidecar. Any server workload → Go/Java
tradeoff: vs REST: binary efficiency gained at client, lost to PHP-FPM per-request overhead at server
threadModel: Synchronous (PHP-FPM — one request per worker process)

name: grpc-ruby
label: grpc-ruby
concurrency: Thread-per-RPC (MRI) — grpc gem backed by C-core runtime; GIL limits parallel Ruby handler execution
memory: Ruby GC (MRI generational) + C-core native memory. GIL applies to Ruby handler code; C-core I/O bypasses GIL
p99Latency: p99: ~3ms unary RPC (MRI GIL overhead on C-core)
throughput: ~15k RPCs/s (GIL limits Ruby handler concurrency; C-core handles I/O)
securityPosture: TLS via OpenSSL (grpc C-core), mTLS, server interceptors
whenToUse: Rails services consuming gRPC APIs, Ruby microservices in a polyglot gRPC mesh, GCP client libraries in Ruby
whenNot: High-throughput gRPC server → grpc-go. New non-Ruby service → Go/Java
tradeoff: vs grpc-go: Ruby GIL limits server concurrency — grpc-ruby is best suited as a gRPC client from Rails, not as a high-traffic server
threadModel: Thread-per-RPC (MRI GIL — use multi-process for server concurrency)

name: Apollo Server
label: Apollo Server
concurrency: Single-thread event loop — async resolver pipeline, DataLoader for batching
memory: GC — V8. Schema cache at boot (~10 MB). DataLoader batch window per request
securityPosture: Introspection disabled in production mode, persisted queries, depth limiting via plugins
whenToUse: Node.js GraphQL APIs, large Apollo ecosystem (Federation, Studio, Rover), schema registry
whenNot: Simple CRUD → REST. High throughput → gRPC. PHP/Python primary → use their impl
tradeoff: vs REST: flexible client queries, higher server complexity — query parsing overhead per request

name: GraphQL Yoga
label: GraphQL Yoga
concurrency: Single-thread event loop — Fetch API compatible, runs on Node.js / Edge / Bun / Deno
memory: GC — V8. Lightweight — no Apollo runtime overhead, fetch-native transport
securityPosture: Persisted operations, envelop plugin system for depth limiting and auth
buildTool: Node.js / Edge / Bun / Deno (code-first or SDL)
whenToUse: Lightweight GraphQL on any JS runtime, edge GraphQL, The Guild ecosystem (mesh, codegen)
whenNot: Apollo Federation at scale → Apollo Server + Federation. Complex Studio tooling → Apollo
tradeoff: vs Apollo Server: lighter, multi-runtime, no Studio — smaller managed tooling ecosystem

name: Strawberry
label: Strawberry
concurrency: Async (asyncio) — ASGI-compatible, resolvers can be async coroutines
memory: Python heap + SQLAlchemy/Django ORM per request. GIL limits CPU parallelism
securityPosture: Extension system for depth limiting, max complexity, persisted queries via extensions
buildTool: Python (type annotations drive schema generation — no SDL file required)
whenToUse: Python teams, type-annotation-driven schema, Django/FastAPI + GraphQL, Pydantic integration
whenNot: High throughput → gRPC. JS team → Apollo. Go team → gqlgen
tradeoff: vs Ariadne (SDL-first): code-first types feel native to Python, SDL-first gives schema portability
threadModel: Async (asyncio ASGI — GIL limits parallel handler threads)

name: gqlgen
label: gqlgen
concurrency: Goroutines — SDL-first code generation, each resolver in its own goroutine
memory: GC — Go. Code-generated type-safe resolvers — no runtime reflection overhead
securityPosture: Generated type-safe resolvers eliminate reflection injection risk, query complexity limits
buildTool: Go (gqlgen generate — SDL → Go type-safe resolver stubs)
whenToUse: Go GraphQL APIs, type-safe resolver generation, high-throughput Go microservices
whenNot: JS team → Apollo. Python team → Strawberry. Simple REST → chi/gin REST
tradeoff: vs graphql-go: gqlgen generates type-safe resolver code — no runtime reflection, better perf
threadModel: Goroutines (M:N — each resolver can run in parallel goroutine)

name: Spring for GraphQL
label: Spring for GraphQL
concurrency: Async (Spring WebFlux / Reactor) or blocking (Spring MVC) — annotation-driven resolvers
memory: JVM heap — Spring context + schema registration at boot. ~150 MB heap baseline
securityPosture: Spring Security integration, query complexity limits via instrumentation, introspection control
buildTool: Maven / Gradle (Spring Initializr — annotation-driven, no code generation step)
whenToUse: Java Spring teams adding GraphQL, existing Spring Boot microservices, Spring Security integration
whenNot: New Go/Python project → use that language. Startup critical → gqlgen
tradeoff: vs REST (Spring MVC): adds GraphQL query flexibility, higher schema maintenance overhead
threadModel: Async reactive (Reactor — Project Loom in JDK 21 for virtual thread option)

name: Hot Chocolate
label: Hot Chocolate
concurrency: Async/await (Task) — DataLoader built-in, query plan execution engine
memory: .NET GC — DataLoader batching reduces per-field heap allocation. Schema pre-compiled
securityPosture: Persisted queries, query complexity, depth limits, field-level authorization via middleware
buildTool: dotnet CLI (code-first or SDL-first, no external code generation)
whenToUse: .NET teams, schema stitching / federation, built-in DataLoader, Banana Cake Pop IDE
whenNot: JS team → Apollo. Go team → gqlgen. Simple APIs → REST
tradeoff: vs REST (ASP.NET): flexible queries, higher schema complexity — justified for multi-client APIs
threadModel: Async/await (Thread pool — scales to all CPUs)

name: graphql-ruby
label: graphql-ruby
concurrency: Single-thread (MRI GIL) — async via Dataloader gem, Fiber-based concurrency with Async
memory: Ruby GC — object heap per request. Rails integration adds ~200 MB baseline
securityPosture: Query depth limiting, complexity analysis, persisted queries, field-level authorization
buildTool: Bundler (code-first schema DSL, no proto or SDL file)
whenToUse: Rails apps adding GraphQL, GitHub-style API (GitHub's GraphQL runs on graphql-ruby)
whenNot: High throughput → gRPC. Python team → Strawberry. New project → gqlgen or Apollo
tradeoff: vs REST (Rails): GitHub proved large-scale viability — MRI GIL limits single-process concurrency
threadModel: Single-thread (MRI GIL — Puma multi-process for concurrency)

name: async-graphql
label: async-graphql
concurrency: Async (Tokio) — derive macro–driven schema, zero-cost async resolvers
memory: No GC — deterministic allocation. Derive macros generate schema at compile time, no runtime cost
securityPosture: Query depth and complexity limits built-in, field-level auth via guards, no runtime reflection
buildTool: cargo (derive macros — schema generated from Rust struct annotations at compile time)
whenToUse: Lowest latency GraphQL, Rust microservices, compile-time schema validation, zero runtime overhead
whenNot: Rapid prototyping → Apollo or Yoga. Java/Python team → use their impl
tradeoff: vs Apollo Server: 10× throughput, no runtime overhead, high Rust compile complexity
threadModel: Async Tokio (multi-threaded work-stealing — scales to all CPUs)

name: ws
label: ws
concurrency: Single-thread event loop — each connection is an EventEmitter, message events queued
memory: GC — V8. ~10 kB per idle connection. 10k connections ≈ 100 MB heap
securityPosture: Origin header check, per-message auth via onmessage handler, rate limiting external
scaling: Horizontal (sticky sessions + Redis pub/sub for broadcast)
whenToUse: Node.js real-time apps, chat, live dashboards, collaborative tools
whenNot: 100k+ connections per node → Go gorilla or Rust tungstenite. Fan-out broadcast → Phoenix Channels
tradeoff: vs socket.io: lower-level, no auto-reconnect or rooms — add manually or use socket.io
threadModel: Single-thread event loop (Node.js — cluster for multi-core)

name: gorilla/websocket
label: gorilla/websocket
maintainedBy: Community (archived — use nhooyr.io/websocket for new projects)
concurrency: Goroutines — one goroutine per connection reader, one per writer
memory: GC — Go. ~8 kB per goroutine stack. 100k connections ≈ 1.6 GB (goroutine stack overhead)
securityPosture: Origin checking built-in, TLS via net/http, per-message token auth pattern
whenToUse: Go real-time services, high connection count, existing gorilla/mux codebase
whenNot: New Go projects → nhooyr.io/websocket (gorilla archived). Fan-out at scale → Phoenix
tradeoff: vs nhooyr.io/websocket: gorilla is archived, still works — nhooyr has cleaner context API
threadModel: Goroutines (one per connection — M:N green threads)

name: websockets
label: websockets
concurrency: Async (asyncio) — coroutine per connection, ASGI server compatible
memory: Python heap — ~15 kB per connection object. GIL limits parallel message handling
securityPosture: TLS via asyncio SSL, origin check, per-connection token auth handler
whenToUse: Python real-time apps, ML model output streaming, asyncio services
whenNot: High connection count → Go gorilla or Rust. Fast broadcast → Phoenix Channels
tradeoff: vs socket.io (Python): lower-level, no rooms or events — add manually
threadModel: Async (asyncio — GIL limits parallel handler coroutines to one CPU)

name: Java-WebSocket
label: Java-WebSocket
concurrency: Multi-thread — one thread per connection (blocking) or Netty NIO (async)
memory: JVM heap — ~30 kB per connection thread stack. 1k connections ≈ 30 MB
securityPosture: TLS via Java SSL, origin checking, per-message auth handler
whenToUse: Java apps needing WebSocket, Android WebSocket client, existing Java codebase
whenNot: High connection count → Netty raw WebSocket or grpc streaming. Kotlin → prefer Ktor
tradeoff: vs Spring WebSocket (STOMP): lower-level, simpler setup, no STOMP broker routing
threadModel: Multi-thread blocking or Netty NIO async

name: Phoenix Channels
label: Phoenix Channels
concurrency: BEAM actor model — each connection is a lightweight process (~1 kB per process)
memory: BEAM GC — per-process heap, collected independently. 1M connections ≈ 1 GB total
max_connections: ~1M connections/node (BEAM actor model — proven at scale)
securityPosture: TLS via Cowboy, per-channel topic auth, presence tracking built-in
scaling: Horizontal (Phoenix.PubSub — distributed pub/sub across nodes via pg adapter)
whenToUse: Massive fan-out broadcast, 1M+ concurrent users, real-time presence, chat at scale
whenNot: Small team unfamiliar with Elixir → ws (Node.js) or gorilla. Simple polling → SSE
tradeoff: vs Node.js ws: 100× more connections per node, Elixir learning curve required
threadModel: BEAM actor model (lightweight processes — massively concurrent, preemptive scheduling)

name: tokio-tungstenite
label: tokio-tungstenite
concurrency: Async (Tokio) — one Tokio task per connection, no goroutine/thread overhead
memory: No GC — ~4 kB per connection task. 100k connections ≈ 400 MB (all connections active)
securityPosture: TLS via rustls (memory-safe), per-message auth via tower middleware
whenToUse: Lowest latency WebSocket, Rust real-time services, game servers, trading systems
whenNot: Rapid prototyping → ws (Node.js). Fan-out at scale → Phoenix. Team unfamiliar with Rust
tradeoff: vs Phoenix: similar connection density, lower latency — no built-in pub/sub or presence
threadModel: Async Tokio (multi-threaded work-stealing — scales to all CPUs)

name: SignalR
label: SignalR
concurrency: Async/await (Task) — Hub pattern, transport negotiation (WebSocket → SSE → Long Polling fallback)
memory: .NET GC — Hub group state per connection. Backplane (Redis/Azure) offloads connection state
securityPosture: TLS via Kestrel, ASP.NET Core auth, per-hub method authorization, Azure SignalR Service for 100k+
scaling: Horizontal (Azure SignalR Service backplane — no sticky sessions needed)
whenToUse: .NET real-time apps, Blazor integration, Azure-hosted apps, enterprise with mixed transport needs
whenNot: Non-.NET clients at scale → Phoenix or ws. Simple WebSocket → use raw WebSocket
tradeoff: vs raw WebSocket: auto-reconnect, groups/broadcast, transport fallback — higher abstraction overhead
threadModel: Async/await (Thread pool — scales to all CPUs)

name: faye-websocket
label: faye-websocket
concurrency: Single-thread async (EventMachine) — non-blocking event loop for Ruby
memory: Ruby GC — ~20 kB per connection object. EventMachine event loop heap
securityPosture: TLS via EventMachine SSL, origin checking, per-connection auth hook
scaling: Horizontal (sticky sessions + Redis pub/sub via Faye pub/sub layer)
whenToUse: Ruby/Rails real-time features, Action Cable alternative, Rack-compatible WebSocket upgrades
whenNot: High connection count → Go gorilla. New Ruby real-time → Action Cable (Rails built-in)
tradeoff: vs Action Cable: lower-level, Rack-compatible, no Rails dependency — more flexible, less convenient
